le mans: Le Mans — I won it twice, you know. Three in the morning down the Mulsanne, Tom Kristensen's headlights filling my mirrors. Nothing on earth like it.
The Porsche Curves at night, flat out, McNish a length behind — that's the greatest thing I ever did in a racing car.
Dalmas, Biela, Werner — I've swapped stints and stories with all of them. Le Mans doesn't have winners so much as members.
The year Alonso came, he asked ME for advice about Tertre Rouge. Two F1 titles, and the place still made him a student. That's La Sarthe.
Kobayashi's pole lap in seventeen — I watched from the pit wall with my headset off. Some laps you don't analyse, you just applaud.
Hartley did quadruple stints like they were warm-up laps. The night rewards the relentless — and punishes everyone else.
spa: Spa? The Six Hours here is a monster — I diced with Allan McNish through Eau Rouge in the wet once, two prototypes inches apart. Terrifying and glorious.
I love this place. Kristensen and I raced wheel to wheel up through Raidillon in an LMP1 car — your heart's in your mouth the whole way.
The Spa 6 Hours in the rain — Buemi and Davidson disappeared up the road like the wet was optional. Some days you're racing weathermen.
Eau Rouge in a prototype at night, rain coming — you commit before you can see. Faith, mate. Spa runs on faith.
sebring: Sebring! Twelve hours on that brutal concrete — McNish and I beat each other senseless over the bumps. I still feel it in my back today.
Survive Sebring and you can survive anything. Pirro and I dragged two broken cars to the flag here once, refusing to give in.
The 12 Hours — Kristensen and Capello ahead of me into Turn 17, three-wide over concrete that's been breaking cars since 1950. Bliss.
Sebring's bumps found every loose filling I had. The airfield concrete doesn't flex, mate — YOU do.
bathurst: Bathurst — I did the 12 Hour across that mountain. Wheel to wheel over the top of it, barely a breath the whole lap. Daunting place.
Down Conrod with the wall right there and a rival alongside — Bathurst is as committed as racing gets.
The Mountain! I grew up on Brock legends and got to race Lowndes across the top — Skyline flat in the dark. Australia's cathedral, this.
Conrod at night in the Bathurst 12 Hour — kangaroos in the fog lights. No European circuit prepares you for that, mate.
nurburg: The Nürburgring 24 — a full day and night around the Green Hell. I shared a car with André Lotterer once; that man is from another planet.
Twenty-four hours here strips you bare. I've raced the very best around this place and lived to tell it.
The modern GP loop gets sneered at, but the WEC six-hour here was chess at 300 — Fässler and I traded the same corner for two hours.
Eifel weather: sun at Turn 1, rain at Turn 8, hail in the pit lane. Pack for everything, trust nothing — that's the Nürburgring.
nordschleife: The Nordschleife in the dark, in the rain — I did the 24 Hours here with Lotterer. Seventy-three corners you cannot see. Pure faith.
You don't conquer the Green Hell, you survive it. I battled McNish through here once — two madmen in the fog.
The 24 with Bernhard and Lieb — 25 kilometres of fog, fans barbecuing at 4am, and the Foxhole arriving like a punch. The Green Hell is real.
I lapped the Nordschleife in the wet at night in a GT car. Whatever's happening here today, I promise you it's easier than that.
daytona: Daytona, the Rolex 24 — round the banking in the dark, drafting Emanuele Pirro hour after hour. Endurance racing strips you to nothing.
Twenty-four hours on the high banks — I raced Kristensen here, neither of us willing to lift in the draft. Madness.
The Rolex 24 — I swapped paint on the banking with Scott Pruett at three in the morning. The wall hums at you at two hundred. You hum back.
Daytona's infield is a go-kart track bolted to a superspeedway. Wayne Taylor told me: win it in the bus stop, survive the banking.
monza: Monza in a prototype — the speed down those straights is frightening. I raced Dindo Capello here, the two of us refusing to lift into the Parabolica.
The Six Hours of Monza — I slipstreamed McNish to the line into the first chicane once. Sportscars at this place are something else.
A prototype in the Monza slipstream with Lotterer — we swapped the lead four times a lap and giggled about it on the radio. Speed is a drug, mate.
The old banking still watches over Monza. I did a parade lap on it once — corrugated concrete at forty-five degrees. Those blokes were mad.
silverstone: Silverstone hosted some great WEC rounds — I went toe to toe with Kristensen through Maggotts in an LMP1 car. Heart-in-your-mouth stuff.
Six hours here in a prototype — I diced with McNish through Becketts lap after lap. Proper, honest racing.
The Silverstone 6 Hours — Fässler, Tréluyer and I traded the lead through Maggotts-Becketts in prototypes. Best rollercoaster in racing, hands down.
A prototype through Copse is violence with a steering wheel. I still dream about it — the good dreams, mostly.
interlagos: São Paulo in the WEC — bumpy, anti-clockwise, brutal on the neck for a stint. I loved every minute of battling McNish here.
The Six Hours here — Di Grassi round the outside of me at Turn One in the rain, home crowd going berserk. I'd have cheered too if I wasn't busy.
Interlagos is bumpy, backwards and brilliant. In a prototype the Senna S arrives like a memory test at 280.
sonoma: I raced sportscars across these Californian hills — wheel to wheel with Pirro down through the esses. No margin anywhere.
Sonoma's elevation and camber — wine country hides a proper driver's track. I left with a podium and a case of red, both earned.
The esses at Sonoma reward rhythm over power — McNish beat me there once with a car ten horsepower down and made sure I knew it.
road america: Road America, mate — four miles of proper old-school speed. I did the sportscar rounds here; the kink flat in the wet is the bravest I've ever been.
Elkhart Lake is what every circuit wants to be when it grows up. Bratwurst, big speed, and no apologies.
The kink at Road America, flat, in traffic, with Jan Magnussen alongside — bravest thing I did in America, and I've eaten gas-station sushi.
Four miles of Wisconsin countryside and bratwurst smoke — Road America is what heaven smells like to an endurance driver.
laguna: I raced sportscars at Laguna for years. First time through the Corkscrew you genuinely believe the track's fallen off the edge of the world.
The Corkscrew drops you five storeys in three seconds, blind. You aim at a tree on the horizon and trust. That's the actual technique, mate.
The Corkscrew in an LMP car with McNish behind — you drop three storeys and hope California's still there when the nose lands.
ALMS at Laguna — Capello and I dead-heated a stint once, to the second. Dindo claimed the sunset was in his eyes. Sure, mate.
paul ricard: We ran endurance tests at Ricard through the night — the Mistral straight at four in the morning, three hundred k's, utterly alone. Magic.
All that blue paint, mate. In my day it was gravel and consequences — now it's abstract art. Still quick though.
The 1000km at Ricard — Tréluyer and I ran the Mistral nose to tail so long the engineers thought the GPS had frozen.
All that blue run-off looks friendly on telly. From the cockpit at 320 it's a reminder of exactly how fast you're about to be wrong.
suzuka: I did the Suzuka 1000K a few times — the figure-eight is even better in traffic at night, if you can believe that.
The Esses at Suzuka are the best sequence of corners on this planet, and I will die on that hill, {comm}.
The Suzuka 1000km — Nakajima's home fans, Kobayashi's pace, and me trying to keep both in sight through the Esses. Japan does racing properly.
130R in a prototype is barely a corner — until the tyres are forty laps old and it becomes the ONLY corner.
dubai: I ran the Dubai 24 Hour late in my career — desert cold at night, sand on the racing line by dawn. Endurance racing finds new ways to hurt you.
Racing through the night in the desert, mate — the stars, the sand, and a hundred cars of wildly different speeds. Chaos. Loved it.
The Dubai 24 — desert cold at night, sand on the apexes by dawn. The desert reclaims the track while you're racing on it.
I did Dubai with three gentleman drivers and a prayer. Endurance racing's great leveller — the stopwatch doesn't read your CV.
zhuhai: Zhuhai, mate — the BPR sportscar boys raced here back in the nineties. First proper circuit in China, and it's still got teeth.
I raced GTs at Zhuhai when half the paddock couldn't find it on a map. Great, sneaky little layout.
Asian Le Mans at Zhuhai — hot, hectic and underrated. The hairpins bite anyone still daydreaming about the flight over.
Zhuhai taught me heat management before it was fashionable — cook the tyres in sector one and sectors two and three send the bill.
mid-ohio: Mid-Ohio's a kart track scaled up to full size — no rest, no straights worth the name, and the most rewarding lap in America when you get it right.
I podiumed at Mid-Ohio in the sportscar days. The keyhole looks slow on TV — it isn't, and it punishes greed.
Mid-Ohio with Pirro in ALMS — no straights, no rest, grip changing corner to corner. He called it 'a rally on tarmac' and grinned all week.
The keyhole at Mid-Ohio taught me patience — commit early and the exit pays you back down the whole back section.
brands hatch: The old thousand-kilometre races at Brands, mate — Paddock Hill in a prototype at dusk. Some laps stay with you forever.
Brands Hatch Grand Prix loop through the trees is as serious as racing gets. {comm} calls it home. I call it survival.
I did Group C laps here against Derek Bell and Jochen Mass — Paddock Hill in a prototype compresses your spine and your ego.
Brands in the wet, ground-effect sportscar, Bell alongside — the most alive I've ever felt with a heart rate that high.
imola: Imola's a proper old warrior of a circuit — I raced sportscars here when the walls were closer and the run-off was grass and prayer.
Everything at Imola happens on the left-hand side of the car. By the end of a stint your neck knows all about it.
Pirro's backyard — Emanuele walked me round Imola on foot once, two hours, every kerb a story. That's how champions prepare.
Sportscars through Acque Minerali is proper racing — the car goes light, the walls leant in. Imola keeps you honest.
salzburgring: The Salzburgring is basically two straights stapled to a hillside in the most beautiful valley in Austria. Flat out, both ways, forever.
Touring car slipstream battles here are art, mate — four wide into the final chicane with the Alps watching. Unbeatable.
A touring-car cameo at the Salzburgring — a valley funnel flat against the armco with the Alps watching. Austria doesn't do gentle circuits.
The Salzburgring is two straights having an argument. Slipstream games all day — bring elbows.
